title: Write discovery index patterns guide with mutable://open security tradeoffs
markdown:
## Goal

Document the patterns and tradeoffs for building discovery indexes on Firecat, with particular attention to the security implications of using `mutable://open` for public discoverability. Developers building apps where users need to find each other's content (social feeds, marketplaces, directories) will hit this pattern immediately, and the tradeoffs are non-obvious.

## Background

Firecat's URI model means data is addressed, not searched. If Alice writes her profile to `mutable://accounts/{alicePubkey}/profile`, Bob can only read it if he knows Alice's pubkey. There is no built-in search or discovery mechanism.

The current workaround is **discovery indexes**: write a reference to your content at a well-known public URI. For example, an app might maintain a directory at `mutable://open/my-app/users/{pubkey}` that links to each user's profile. Anyone can browse `mutable://open/my-app/users/` to discover all participants.

This works but has critical tradeoffs:
- **`mutable://open` is world-writable**: Anyone can write to any path under `mutable://open`. This means anyone can overwrite, spam, or vandalize the discovery index. There is no authentication.
- **No integrity protection**: Without authentication, a discovery index at `mutable://open` can be poisoned -- an attacker could overwrite legitimate user listings with malicious content.
- **Namespace squatting**: First-writer wins on `mutable://open`. Someone could squat on `mutable://open/my-app/` before the app developer does.
- **Alternative: `mutable://accounts`**: If the app developer signs writes to `mutable://accounts/{appPubkey}/users/...`, the index is authenticated. But this requires the app to hold a signing key and proxy all index writes, which re-centralizes control.

The TASKS.md mentions designing an integrity-protected public namespace (`mutable://verified`) as a longer-term solution, but that does not exist yet. The documentation should explain the current patterns honestly.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A discovery index patterns section exists in `skills/b3nd/APP_COOKBOOK.md` or as a standalone guide
- [ ] The document explains the discovery problem clearly (data is addressed, not searched)
- [ ] At least three patterns are documented:
  - [ ] Pattern 1: Public open index (`mutable://open/my-app/directory/...`) -- simplest, least secure
  - [ ] Pattern 2: App-controlled authenticated index (`mutable://accounts/{appPubkey}/directory/...`) -- more secure, more centralized
  - [ ] Pattern 3: User-published discovery entries (`immutable://accounts/{userPubkey}/my-app/public/...`) -- decentralized, requires listing
- [ ] Each pattern includes: code example, security analysis, recommended use cases
- [ ] The `mutable://open` security tradeoffs are prominently documented:
  - [ ] World-writable nature explained
  - [ ] Attack vectors listed (overwrite, spam, squatting, poisoning)
  - [ ] Mitigation strategies (client-side verification, signed content in open URIs, rate-limiting at app level)
- [ ] The document mentions the future `mutable://verified` concept as a design direction without promising it
- [ ] Guidance on when to use each pattern based on app requirements (e.g., "for a social app, prefer Pattern 3")

## Implementation Guide

1. Read `skills/b3nd/FIRECAT.md` for the complete description of Firecat programs and URI patterns:
   - `mutable://open` -- anyone can write, no auth
   - `mutable://accounts/{pubkey}` -- pubkey-signed, authenticated
   - `immutable://open` -- anyone, write-once
   - `immutable://accounts/{pubkey}` -- pubkey-signed, write-once
2. Read `skills/b3nd/APP_COOKBOOK.md` for any existing discovery patterns
3. Read `skills/b3nd/FAQ.md` for relevant design rationale
4. Design and document three patterns:

   **Pattern 1: Open Directory**
   ```
   URI: mutable://open/my-app/directory/{userId}
   Data: { pubkey: "...", name: "...", joinedAt: ... }
   ```
   - Anyone can add themselves
   - Anyone can overwrite anyone else's entry (security risk)
   - Mitigation: include a signature in the data so readers can verify authenticity

   **Pattern 2: App-Controlled Index**
   ```
   URI: mutable://accounts/{appPubkey}/directory/{userId}
   Data: { pubkey: "...", name: "...", verified: true }
   ```
   - Only the app can write (requires app signing key)
   - Centralized control -- the app is a gatekeeper
   - Suitable for curated directories

   **Pattern 3: User-Published Discovery**
   ```
   URI: immutable://accounts/{userPubkey}/my-app/public/profile
   Data: { name: "...", bio: "..." }
   ```
   - Each user writes their own discovery entry
   - Authenticated (only the user can write to their namespace)
   - Discovery requires listing `immutable://accounts/*/my-app/public/profile` which is not supported (you need to know pubkeys or have a bootstrap list)

5. For each pattern, write:
   - Complete code example (create, read, list)
   - Security analysis (who can write, who can read, attack vectors)
   - When to use this pattern
   - Limitations

6. Write the "mutable://open security tradeoffs" section with specific attack scenarios and mitigations
7. Add the guide to `skills/b3nd/APP_COOKBOOK.md` as a new "Discovery Patterns" section

## Files to Create/Modify

- `skills/b3nd/APP_COOKBOOK.md` -- add discovery index patterns section with security analysis

## Dependencies

- **Depends on**: Nothing -- this documents patterns using existing protocol capabilities
- **Blocks**: Nothing directly, but this content feeds into G4-F2-02 (developer guides) and G4-F3-01 (builder pitch)
tasksExcerpt:
- [ ] Write privacy warning for "private" visibility in FIRECAT.md and SDK docs
- [ ] Document record size limits
- [ ] Document `send()` atomicity behavior
- [ ] Write "KeyBundle" recipe (backup, multi-device sync, recovery)
- [ ] Write standard encrypted write pattern documentation
- [ ] Write discovery index patterns guide (with `mutable://open` security tradeoffs)
- [ ] Create developer quickstart for building a Firecat app (end-to-end, from key creation to deployed app)
